title: Shepherds, Humility, and Final Greetings
subtitle: Elders shepherd the flock, all clothe themselves with humility, and resist the devil who prowls

heb.paragraph: Elders among you - the term denotes both age and office. Peter addresses them as a fellow elder and witness of Christ sufferings.
Shepherd the flock of God - the same verb Jesus used to Peter at the restoration (John 21:16). Pastoral care is the elder primary duty.
The unfading crown of glory - the reward contrasts with earthly wreaths that wither. The Chief Shepherd dispenses eternal honor.

panels.ctx: Peter exhorts elders as a fellow elder and witness of Christ sufferings, a partaker of coming glory. Shepherd the flock - exercising oversight not under compulsion but willingly, not for shameful gain but eagerly, not domineering but as examples. When the Chief Shepherd appears, you will receive the unfading crown of glory. Likewise, younger ones should submit to elders.

cross.note: Jesus said to Peter: Feed my lambs, tend my sheep, feed my sheep.
Pay careful attention to yourselves and to all the flock, in which the Holy Spirit has made you overseers.
Son of man, prophesy against the shepherds of Israel - the indictment of false shepherds.
They do it to receive a perishable wreath, but we an imperishable.

notes.note: I exhort the elders as a fellow elder and witness of Christ sufferings. Shepherd the flock - not under compulsion but willingly, not for gain but eagerly.
Not domineering over those in your charge but being examples. When the Chief Shepherd appears, you will receive the unfading crown of glory.
Likewise, you who are younger, be subject to the elders.
Calvin notes three contrasts in pastoral ministry: compulsion vs. willingness, greed vs. eagerness, domination vs. example. The Chief Shepherd evaluates all under-shepherds.
Younger believers owe respect to elders. The order of the church requires mutual submission under appropriate authority.
Peter identifies himself as sympresbyteros (fellow elder), not as apostle. He leads by identification, not rank.
The Chief Shepherd (archipoimenos) is Christ. The term is unique in the NT and emphasizes Christ authority over all pastors.
Schreiner notes that Peter example-based leadership contrasts with coercive power. Shepherding is service, not rule; example, not command.
Jobes emphasizes that Peter speaks as a fellow elder, not as a superior. His authority is pastoral, not hierarchical.

heb.paragraph: Humble yourselves under the mighty hand of God - active humility, not passive resignation. God exalts the humble at the proper time.
Cast all your anxieties on him, for he cares for you - the transfer of burden from believer to God. Divine care grounds human peace.
Resist the devil, firm in your faith - active opposition through faithful perseverance. The devil prowls like a roaring lion seeking to devour.

panels.ctx: Clothe yourselves with humility toward one another - God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble (Proverbs 3:34). Humble yourselves under God mighty hand, that he may exalt you at the proper time. Cast all anxieties on him, for he cares for you. Be sober-minded, watchful - your adversary the devil prowls like a roaring lion seeking someone to devour. Resist him, firm in faith, knowing that the same sufferings are being experienced by your brotherhood throughout the world. After you have suffered a little while, the God of all grace who called you to eternal glory in Christ will himself restore, confirm, strengthen, and establish you. To him be dominion forever.

cross.note: Toward the scorners he is scornful, but to the humble he gives favor.
God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble. Submit to God. Resist the devil, and he will flee.
Cast your burden on the LORD, and he will sustain you.
Put on the whole armor of God, that you may be able to stand against the schemes of the devil.

notes.note: Humble yourselves under God mighty hand. Cast all your anxieties on him, for he cares for you.
Be sober, watchful. Your adversary the devil prowls like a roaring lion. Resist him, firm in faith.
After you have suffered a little while, the God of all grace will restore, confirm, strengthen, establish you. To him be dominion forever.
Calvin links humility and anxiety-casting: pride leads to self-reliance and worry; humility leads to God-reliance and peace.
The lion image is ferocity, not stealth. The devil attacks openly through persecution. Resistance is through faith, not flight.
The verb cast (epiripsantes) is aorist participle - a decisive transfer. Anxiety belongs to God, not the believer.
The roaring lion image may evoke Psalm 22:13 or simply illustrate predatory danger. The devil seeks vulnerable prey.
Schreiner emphasizes the eschatological frame: suffering is for a little while; glory is eternal. God will complete what he began.
Jobes notes the paradox: resist the devil by being humble and casting anxiety on God. Aggressive resistance is grounded in dependent faith.

heb.paragraph: By Silvanus, a faithful brother as I regard him - Silvanus likely served as secretary (amanuensis). The polished Greek may reflect his contribution.
She who is at Babylon sends greetings - almost certainly Rome, using the code name common in Jewish and Christian apocalyptic.
Greet one another with the kiss of love - the holy kiss was a sign of Christian fellowship and unity.

panels.ctx: Peter concludes: By Silvanus, a faithful brother, I have written briefly, exhorting and declaring that this is the true grace of God - stand firm in it. She who is at Babylon sends greetings, and so does Mark my son. Greet one another with the kiss of love. Peace to all of you who are in Christ.

cross.note: Judas called Barsabbas, and Silas, leading men among the brothers.
Get Mark and bring him with you, for he is very useful to me for ministry.
Greet one another with a holy kiss.
Babylon the great, mother of prostitutes - Rome as Babylon in Revelation.

notes.note: By Silvanus, a faithful brother, I have written briefly. This is the true grace of God; stand firm in it.
She at Babylon (the church at Rome) greets you, and so does Mark my son. Greet one another with the kiss of love. Peace to all in Christ.
Calvin takes stand firm as the letter summary. Amidst suffering, believers must not waver. The true grace of God is their foundation.
Babylon is Rome - the exile location of God people. Peter writes from the center of empire to scattered believers.
The phrase by Silvanus (dia Silouanou) could mean Silvanus delivered the letter or helped compose it. The latter is more likely given the Greek style.
Mark my son indicates spiritual relationship, not physical. Mark is traditionally linked to Peter as his interpreter.
Schreiner notes that the closing summarizes the letter: this is the true grace of God. Grace enables standing firm under trial.
Jobes emphasizes the network of relationships: Silvanus, Mark, the church at Rome, the churches of Asia Minor - a connected community of suffering saints.

rows.label: vv. 1-5
rows.text: Shepherd the flock; elders and younger

lit.note: Chapter 5 addresses church leadership (vv. 1-5), spiritual warfare (vv. 6-11), and closes with personal greetings (vv. 12-14). The letter ends as it began: grace and peace.

themes.note: Sovereignty dominates: God mighty hand, God care, God restoration. Faith is the means of resistance. Mission appears in the network of churches and co-workers.
